artifacts.content:
Hi Hel,

Kids are finally in bed so I’ve got ten quiet minutes before I crash. Thought I’d write instead of just sending another rushed text.

I’m still pushing ahead with the two‑lot Torrens Title subdivision at 12 Bangalley Street – Lot 23A with the existing house and the new rear Lot 23B off that 4.0 m driveway along the eastern boundary. I know you’re sick of hearing about stormwater pits and easements, but it’s the only way I can see to untangle some of the mess we’re in.

Mainly, I can’t keep ignoring the $68,000 we still owe from Dad’s lung cancer treatment and palliative care at Royal North Shore Hospital between March 2020 and when he died in August 2021, plus the funeral costs. Selling the back lot once the subdivision certificate is through would actually clear that, and still let us keep the front house as a proper base for Mum. She’s wobblier on her feet, and the depression since Dad’s stroke and then the cancer has never really lifted. I can’t see her coping with another move.

On top of that, Emily and I have about $120k in unsecured card and personal loan debt from 2019–2023, between her unpaid postnatal depression leave from Northern Beaches Private and my three‑month unpaid suspension from Harbourline in mid‑2022 when they did that conflict‑of‑interest investigation on the Brookvale subdivision. We’re just treading water with minimums. Being able to sell Lot 23B or at least refinance against it would take such a weight off.

Day‑to‑day life is still the usual chaos – Sophie obsessing over high school options, Oliver only caring about Saturday soccer, Emily juggling her clinic days and school visits. I’m still doing Sunday runs to Narrabeen to sort Mum’s bills and change light bulbs.

Anyway, didn’t mean to dump all of that, but you of all people get the full picture. How are Lucy and Tom going with school?

Love,
Dan

metadata.subject: Why I’m still pushing the subdivision

artifacts.content:
Hi Emily,

Just got back from site at Frenchs Forest and needed to get this down while it’s in my head.

If we stay on track with the civil works, we should be able to get the subdivision certificate for Bangalley through this year. That means 2 lots formally created – Lot 23A with the existing dwelling, and Lot 23B as a vacant rear lot off the new shared driveway. Once Lot 23B is on its own title, we’ve got a few options.

One is the boring but sensible one: sell it in early 2026. Based on what Josh is seeing with comparable sales, we might get somewhere around $1.6 million. That would wipe the ~$95k in credit card and personal loan debt that’s crept up since Dad died in 2021 and Mum started needing about $1,200 a month for her Parkinson’s treatment at Northern Beaches Hospital. We could also finally stop leaning on the offset account every time something goes wrong with the car.

The other is to hold it and use it as security for a construction loan in 2026 if Helen really does need to get out for good after the family violence AVO hearing at Manly Local Court in October this year. A small secondary dwelling for her, Lucy and Tom out the back would keep them close without everyone being on top of each other.

I know it feels like everything in our life is tied to that block of land, but it kind of is. At least the boring engineering bits are done – separate water, sewer, electricity and NBN conduits already in, and the driveway built to Council spec.

Let’s talk properly after the kids are in bed tonight.

Love,
Dan

metadata.subject: Rear lot options – numbers in one place

artifacts.content:
Hi Mike,

Thanks again for the chat after Lucy’s game on Saturday. It helps having someone who actually understands the legal side of all this.

We finally signed the updated wills at Carter & Lowe Solicitors in Dee Why back in July 2024, but I don’t think I’ve properly explained to you how that ties in with the Avalon subdivision. Once the two‑lot Torrens Title setup is registered – Lot 23A at the front with the existing house, Lot 23B as the vacant rear lot – the plan is:

• Lot 23A stays in Emily’s and my names while we’re alive and is earmarked for Sophie under the will.
• Lot 23B will eventually go into a discretionary trust for Oliver after he turns 18, partly to give him something solid and partly to keep it quarantined if Mum’s early‑stage dementia gets to the point where Mona Vale Hospital’s aged care facility becomes inevitable.

Complicating all of this is the estate dispute bubbling away. Helen and I were blindsided in November 2024 when we got notified that our cousin is contesting Dad’s will in the NSW Supreme Court – trying to get a piece of 12 Bangalley Street and what’s left of Dad’s investments. The rough estimate we’ve been given is around $45,000 in legal fees if it runs its course. That’s another reason I’m keen to have Lot 23B as a separate, clean asset we can either sell or borrow against if we have to.

In the middle of that, normal life goes on – Sophie nagging about a phone upgrade, Oliver demanding I practice corners with him in the dark, Emily buried in reports. It’s surreal balancing kids’ homework with affidavits.

If you see any glaring holes in what we’re trying to do, please shout.

Cheers,
Dan

metadata.subject: How the Avalon subdivision fits into the estate/will stuff

artifacts.content:
Personal notes – Bangalley subdivision + family stuff

1. Site details / basics
- Address: 12 Bangalley Street, Avalon Beach NSW 2107
- Lot 23 in DP 1256789, Torrens Title.
- No boundary adjustment / consolidation / strata / stratum – just a straight two‑lot residential subdivision.
- Existing dwelling to remain on proposed Lot 23A (front), new vacant residential lot as proposed Lot 23B (rear) with access via 4.0 m wide driveway on eastern side.
- All subdivision consent conditions for DA2023/1456 to be ticked off: stormwater drainage, separate water/sewer/electricity/NBN to both lots, driveway and turning area to Council spec, easements for services and right‑of‑carriageway registered.

2. Financial drivers (don’t forget the “why”):
- Clear remaining ~$68k palliative care + funeral debt from Dad’s lung cancer treatment at Royal North Shore Hospital (Mar 2020–Aug 2021).
- Deal with ~$120k unsecured debt from 2019–2023 (Emily’s unpaid postnatal depression leave from Northern Beaches Private + my three‑month unpaid suspension from Harbourline in mid‑2022 over conflict‑of‑interest on another subdivision job).
- Ongoing costs: Mum’s Parkinson’s treatment at Northern Beaches Hospital (~$1,200/month) and likely future dementia/aged care at Mona Vale.

3. Family planning / risk management:
- Long‑term idea: Lot 23A to Sophie, Lot 23B into trust for Oliver after 18 (per July 2024 wills at Carter & Lowe).
- Backup plan: use Lot 23B as collateral for a small dwelling for Helen, Lucy and Tom if the Manly Local Court AVO hearing in Oct 2024 goes badly and her separation from Michael is permanent.

4. Work admin:
- Log my design hours carefully – Harbourline “training exercise” arrangement re: coding some Bangalley work to Brookvale job (June–Aug 2024) could bite me again if not transparent this time.

Remember: this is about stability for everyone, not just squeezing value out of the block.

Hi Mum,

Just wanted to put some thoughts in writing so you don’t have to remember everything we talk about over Sunday dinners.

We’re still on track to split 12 Bangalley Street into 2 lots – Lot 23A at the front with our house, and Lot 23B at the back. Nothing changes for you day‑to‑day: you’ll still come over on Sundays, same kitchen, same veggie patch, same noisy grandkids.

The idea is that Lot 23A stays as the family home for now, and Lot 23B gives us flexibility. We might sell it in a year or two to clear the debts that built up after Dad died in 2021 and when your Parkinson’s treatment at Northern Beaches Hospital started costing around $1,200 a month. If the market is kind, a sale around early 2026 could also give us a buffer for your care if the early dementia gets worse and Mona Vale Hospital’s aged care facility becomes necessary.

There’s also the Helen situation. If the family violence AVO matter at Manly Local Court in October doesn’t go her way and she needs to leave Michael for good, we’re thinking about using Lot 23B as security for a small place out the back for her, Lucy and Tom. Nothing is locked in, but it helps me sleep knowing we have options.

On top of all that, there’s this estate dispute our cousin has kicked off in the NSW Supreme Court over Dad’s will and the Avalon place. The lawyers think it could cost around $45,000 to fight, which is another reason having a separate rear lot might be useful.

Try not to worry about the details. My job is to juggle the engineering and the paperwork; your job is to enjoy Sunday roasts and boss the kids around.

Love you,
Dan

metadata.subject: What the subdivision really means for you

artifacts.content:
Event: Family Court mediation – property/guardianship planning

Time: Thursday 16 October 2025, 9:00 a.m. – 1:00 p.m.
Location: Sydney Federal Circuit and Family Court – mediation rooms
Attendees: Daniel & Emily McAllister, mediator Karen Douglas

Notes:
- This isn’t about us separating – Karen’s helping us get ahead of things while we’re still on good terms, so that if anything ever happens to one or both of us, Sophie and Oliver are looked after properly.
- Need to bring:
  • Copy of July 2024 wills from Carter & Lowe Solicitors (Dee Why office).
  • Draft plan showing proposed Lot 23A (front) and Lot 23B (rear) at 12 Bangalley Street – 2 lots total.
  • Rough figures on potential sale value of Lot 23B in early 2026 (~$1.6m) and current debts (~$95k) plus Mum’s ongoing Parkinson’s and dementia‑related costs (Northern Beaches Hospital + likely Mona Vale aged care).
- Discussion points with Karen:
  • How to tie the rear lot into Sophie and Oliver’s guardianship arrangements – e.g., rear lot as a “clean asset” held in trust for them if both of us are gone.
  • Whether leaving Lot 23A to Sophie and Lot 23B in trust for Oliver (per current wills) still makes sense given Helen’s situation (possible move after Manly Local Court AVO hearing in Oct 2024) and Mum’s health.
  • Any risk around the ongoing NSW Supreme Court estate dispute with our cousin over Dad’s will and the Avalon property.

Reminder to self: organise school pick‑ups – ask Josh if he can grab Oliver from soccer training that afternoon.

metadata.title: Family Court mediation – kids & property plan
metadata.location: Sydney Federal Circuit and Family Court

artifacts.content:
Hi [Lawyer Name],

Thanks for your time in the CBD office on 3 February. I wanted to summarise my understanding of the subdivision/asset‑protection strategy so I don’t muddle it.

We’re proceeding with a straightforward two‑lot subdivision of 12 Bangalley Street – proposed Lot 23A (front) with the existing dwelling and proposed Lot 23B (rear) as a vacant lot. All the servicing is already in: separate water, sewer, electricity and NBN conduits, plus the 4.0 m wide concrete driveway along the eastern boundary.

As you recommended at Carter & Lowe, the plan is to keep Lot 23A in joint names, but have Lot 23B transferred into Emily’s name only from settlement in November 2025. The aim, as we discussed, is to ring‑fence the rear lot from any potential civil action that might one day arise from my old drink‑driving incident on Mona Vale Road back in 2019. I know nothing has surfaced yet, but I’d rather be cautious.

Complicating the picture are a few other factors:

- The informal family arrangement we made at Avalon RSL on 15 March 2023, where Mum said she couldn’t keep helping Helen with her gambling debts and we floated the idea of eventually passing Lot 23B to Helen (possibly July 2025) if her situation stabilises.
- The internal issues at Harbourline – my three‑month unpaid suspension in mid‑2022 over the conflict‑of‑interest investigation on another subdivision – which left us with about $120k in unsecured debt.
- The new NSW Supreme Court estate dispute our cousin has launched over Dad’s will, which could chew up ~$45k in legal fees.

Given all that, can you confirm that putting Lot 23B solely in Emily’s name is still the best course?

Regards,
Daniel McAllister

metadata.subject: Follow-up on subdivision and title structure
